cards.back: Local Area Network — a network covering a small geographical area (e.g. a school or office), typically owned by a single organisation and connected by Ethernet or Wi-Fi.
Wide Area Network — a network covering a large geographical area (city, country, or world). The internet is the world's largest WAN.

All devices connect to a central switch. Data passes through the switch to its destination.
Advantage: one cable failure only affects that device.
Disadvantage: if the switch fails, the whole network fails.

All devices connect to a single backbone cable. Data travels to all devices but is accepted only by the addressed one.
Advantage: cheap and simple.
Disadvantage: backbone failure brings down the whole network.

Devices connect to multiple other devices. In a full mesh, every device connects to every other.
Advantage: highly resilient — data can reroute around failures.
Disadvantage: expensive, complex cabling.
Connects different networks together (e.g. a LAN to the internet). Directs data packets between networks using routing tables.

cards.front: What is the difference between a switch and a hub?
cards.back:
Switch: sends data only to the intended device — more efficient.
Hub: broadcasts data to ALL connected devices — less efficient, creates unnecessary traffic.
Wireless Access Point — allows wireless (Wi-Fi) devices to connect to a wired network.
Transmission Control Protocol — ensures reliable, ordered delivery of data. It breaks data into segments, numbers them, checks for errors, and requests retransmission of lost segments.
Internet Protocol — handles addressing and routing of packets across networks. Every device has an IP address that identifies it.

cards.front: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
cards.back:
HTTP: transfers web pages unencrypted.
HTTPS: HTTP with TLS/SSL encryption — protects data from interception (man-in-the-middle attacks).

cards.front: What is DNS and what does it do?
cards.back: Domain Name System — translates human-readable domain names (e.g. bbc.co.uk) into IP addresses that computers can use to route data.

cards.front: What is the difference between POP3 and IMAP?
cards.back:
POP3: downloads emails to your device and deletes them from the server. Works offline but emails are on one device only.
IMAP: reads emails from the server; they stay synced across all devices.
Breaking data into small packets that travel independently across a network, potentially via different routes, and are reassembled at the destination.

cards.front: Name four fields in a TCP/IP packet.
cards.back:
1. Source IP address
2. Destination IP address
3. Sequence number
4. Checksum (error detection)
(Also: TTL, protocol, payload)

cards.front: What are the four layers of the TCP/IP model?
cards.back:
1. Application (HTTP, SMTP, DNS)
2. Transport (TCP, UDP)
3. Internet (IP)
4. Network Access (Ethernet, Wi-Fi)

cards.front: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
cards.back:
TCP: reliable, ordered, error-checked — used for web browsing, email.
UDP: fast, no delivery guarantee — used for live video/audio streaming, online gaming.
A 32-bit address written as four decimal numbers separated by dots, e.g. 192.168.1.1. Allows approximately 4.3 billion unique addresses.
IPv4 was running out of addresses (~4.3 billion). IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses (written in hex), providing ~340 undecillion unique addresses — essentially unlimited.

cards.front: How do routers decide which path to send a packet?
cards.back: Using routing tables, which assign a cost (metric) to each possible path. Lower cost = preferred route. Costs consider hops, link speed, and congestion.
